items.q: What is the difference between a solar eclipse and a lunar eclipse?
items.correct: In a solar eclipse the Moon blocks the Sun; in a lunar eclipse Earth's shadow covers the Moon.
items.misconception: Solar eclipses happen by day, lunar at night - that is the only difference.
items.why: Students give a superficial answer. The mechanism differs: solar = Moon between Sun and Earth (we're in Moon's shadow); lunar = Earth between Sun and Moon (Moon enters Earth's shadow).

items.q: Why is Jupiter classified as a gas giant?
items.correct: Its bulk is mostly hydrogen and helium with no defined solid surface.
items.misconception: It is mostly air like Earth's atmosphere.
items.why: Students project Earth's atmosphere onto Jupiter. Jupiter has no rocky surface - it's gas transitioning to metallic hydrogen with depth. Very different from a thin atmosphere around a solid planet.

items.q: Why don't we have a solar eclipse every new moon?
items.correct: The Moon's orbit is tilted, so it usually passes above or below the Sun's apparent position.
items.why: Students think every new moon should eclipse the Sun. The Moon's orbit is tilted about 5 degrees to the ecliptic, so usually the new Moon doesn't align - alignment requires nodes lining up, giving eclipse seasons.

items.q: Which moon phase allows a solar eclipse?
items.why: Correct answer: New moon. “Full moon” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: Umbra. “Penumbra” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: By approximately how many degrees is the Moon's orbit tilted from the ecliptic?
items.why: The real figure is 5. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
